Clinical reasoning: Patients needing supplemental oxygen are more likely to require urgent review; it has one of the strongest associations with lower ESI acuity level.
Low oxygen saturation directly signals hypoxia and respiratory compromise, which should influence triage urgency.
Older adults have higher risk of deterioration and admission; age shows the strongest demographic association with ESI.
Chest pain may represent acute coronary syndrome and is one of the most common high-acuity complaints.
Shortness of breath can indicate respiratory failure, heart failure, asthma/COPD exacerbation or sepsis.
Tachycardia or bradycardia can indicate shock, arrhythmia, pain, fever or physiological stress.
Abnormal glucose can indicate diabetic emergencies or severe metabolic illness.
Altered mental status is clinically high risk and associated with urgent neurologic, toxicologic, septic or metabolic causes.
Respiratory rate is an early marker of deterioration and respiratory/metabolic stress.
Suicidal ideation is a safety-critical presentation requiring urgent mental health risk assessment.
